text:
In France there is an effort to coordinate volunteer efforts for making the data more accessible: <https://bzg.fr/covid19-developpeurs-datascientistes-comment-aider/>
Maybe some people in this group could use this to show off their developments!

attachments.title: COVID19: développeurs/datascientistes - comment aider ?
attachments.text: Cette page est là pour aider les développeurs et datascientistes qui souhaient mettre leur compétence au service de la diffusion d'informations concernant les contaminations au COVID19 (Coronavirus) en France. Les projets recensés s'appuient tous sur des données officielles des Agences régionales de santé, des préfecteurs ou de Santé Publique France. Signaler d'autres ressources ou comptes à suivre <mailto:bzg@bzg.fr|bzg@bzg.fr> / @bzg2 (Twitter) / @bzg (Mastodon) / @bzg:<http://matrix.org|matrix.org> Cette page est destinée à circuler largement : pour une mise à jour régulière, consultez ce guide de contribution.

elements.text: In France there is an effort to coordinate volunteer efforts for making the data more accessible:

text:
> There just isn’t that much cross communication between apps that makes sense.
I have a hard time believing this in a fundamental way. Perhaps with the architecture we currently have, but I think a lot of the time, despite using different data structures, we’re talking about the same _things_. Like, a comment on youtube is the same thing as a comment on facebook, even though there is no sense in which they can be interoperated on.
I agree that the market has not borne out the utility of a single structure, but I think of this more as a local vs. global optimum kind of problem.
Wrt applescript, I don’t have any familiarity with it, but I think a solution that requires that developers implement support for it is doomed to fail. What I imagine working is a system where interoperability is the default. But this makes for a less viable business, as it’s harder to lock in your customers (though, I think this difficulty is desirable from an engineering/social perspective).

text: Yeah a lot of my problems with programming exist 'between programs', not just inside them.

text:
I think looking at the success of zapier and ifttt among other tools shows that there is actually a market for cross-app communication. More over, I think "sharing" and "import/export" is usually another big primary use case which has been solved pretty nicely these days.

Also webhooks as a premise is pretty successful for many developers as a tool for connecting entire companies.

text: Hi Reilly, I'm also a huge fan of the IOP talk and am also a professional .NET developer (largely by choice: I believe in the concept of a "common language runtime") and I'd like to hear about what you worked on during your sabbatical.

elements.text: Hi Reilly, I'm also a huge fan of the IOP talk and am also a professional .NET developer (largely by choice: I believe in the concept of a "common language runtime") and I'd like to hear about what you worked on during your sabbatical.

text:
Cool, glad to know I'm not the only .NET person here :)

My sabbatical was... all over the place. A mix of seeking "the one true programming environment" and pursuing personal interests.

I've always enjoyed databases, spent a lot of time catching up and refreshing my memory of database internals (CMU's Andy Pavlo posts his lectures on YouTube, he's fantastic). That got me thinking I'd contribute to Postgres, but I quickly remembered how much I dislike working in C. That led me to build some toy projects in Rust, which I loved in many ways but it's not very amenable to IOP-style continuous feedback (and sometimes I just want a garbage collector, y'know?).

Also rewrote my website, played with Scala, set up a Raspberry Pi home server, and intentionally spent time improving my *nix and Windows CLI tools+habits.

Also rewrote my website, played with Scala, set up a Raspberry Pi home server, and intentionally spent time improving my *nix and Windows CLI tools+habits.

text: I'd be very curious to hear whether you have any tips+tricks for attaining a tight feedback loop in .NET.

elements.text: I'd be very curious to hear whether you have any tips+tricks for attaining a tight feedback loop in .NET.

text: I've been considering shelling out for an NCrunch license. I like the idea of immediate+automatic testing, and I've heard good things about it. But the .NET approach to unit testing (only test public methods, put all your tests in a separate test project) is not very good for exploratory/initial development, so that's probably not the whole solution

text: Hmm, I'm not super happy with the speed of my feedback loop... I rely on unit tests and C# Interactive, but build time is several seconds which is several seconds more than I would like. Resharper can skip building dependencies of X if X's public interface hasn't changed, but I don't have it at home. I'm not familiar with NCrunch, but it sounds like it does something similar.

text: I don't like the "separate test library" thing either. I actually put a mini unit test library inside my Loyc.Essentials library so I could put unit tests in things without needing explicit reference to a unit-testing library. I recently tried to write a Visual Studio unit test runner for it, but I had trouble figuring out how, and gave up. Will probably try again later..

text: I like data structures - I made an in-memory B+ tree for JS and C# structures similar to B+ rtees called A-Lists (<http://core.loyc.net/collections/>). I was reading about Software Transactional Memory the other day. But I can't say I like databases. The relational model bothers me, the impedance mismatch bothers me, the usual separation of database from application bothers me (I hate the idea that my app won't work without a separate step to install the database software and/or the database), discarding the file model bothers me (one does not simply store a database in a folder). The differences between DBs under <http://ADO.NET|ADO.NET> bothers me (they all implement the same interface IDbWhatever but are totally incompatible anyway?!)

text:
Just took a look at Loyc/EC#. Very cool, I could see myself using the shorthand class definition and quick binding operator often.

I've tried to use C# Interactive a few times but it's never stuck. Not sure exactly why.

It occurs to me that I should probably give LINQPad another try now that I'm using Windows as my main OS.

text:
Oh yeah, I did buy LinqPad4. Use it sometimes when I remember to, but it doesn't support new versions of C#. So I assume if I bought LinqPad5/6 it would not support C# 9. But don't listen to me I'm a cheapskate.

I guess I use C# interactive as the replacement, which is fine except that it can't save snippets. And "Initialize interactive with project" is a super important feature that is present in .NET Framework projects but has been missing in .NET Core projects for years. Almost as if the .NET Core people don't use Visual Studio and are on VS Code?

text: For scripting, I feel like the primary challenge is having robust view-model, a direct way to talk about entities that you see on the screen.  It's frustrating because we come close in some sense — I mean we can talk about buttons and lists and menus — but not quite enough to automate things.  I'm reminded of extracting text from PDFs.

text:
My favorite comment there:

> Morty: Aw, geez, Rick, don't you think we ought to abstract away the low-level details here?
> 
> Rick: Listen, Morty. Just _bburpecause_ you wrap something in an abstraction doesn't mean the complexity "goes away". It just means you've put off dealing with it -- usually until it fails. And when it does fail, you now have another layer of shit to dig through to find the root cause. Walk into the fire, Morty. Embrace the suck. And think about how you will handle error conditions before you write the happy path.
:rolling_on_the_floor_laughing: <https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=22599953#22601623> goes on my HN favorites (<https://news.ycombinator.com/favorites?id=akkartik&comments=t>)

text: On business side, RPA, “robotic process automation”, is trending and perhaps becoming a billion dollar industry. It is essentially about using macros, scripting and screen-scraping to automate flows that span over multiple applications. So there is definitely demand in enterprises to get siloed apps to communicate without human intervention.

text: The reason for all the screen scraping is that web app makers have no intention of facilitating database operations on their private data. In the old days companies facilitated cross-program communication, and it was considered essential for all apps to support open, close, print, etc., as basic operations. If you didn't support those you were considered incomplete.

text: <@UKDM3GLAJ> Re AppleScript, for the record, developers implementing it was not a problem early on. Almost all of the important Mac apps were scriptable in the early 2000s. The problem is, and always has been, that just too few people use it.

text:
> After all, each project has its own data structures which is really what isolates them; many programs don't agree on how to store numbers, which is a rather major problem if you are talking about intra-program communication.
I think this is a symptom rather than a cause. Apps don't have shared data structures because people aren't interested in scripting apps.

But Zapier/IFTTT are an interesting counterexample, showing there is a market for this type of technology. This makes me think that the maybe the reason AppleScript isn't used more widely is just, we already have a way of scripting computers locally: Shell scripting languages. Either your app has first-class support for being scripted from the terminal or no one cares. Zapier and IFTTT are popular because there wasn't a default way to do the things they let you do before they came along. Maybe?

I'm speculating here, frankly I'm baffled as to why AppleScript isn't more popular. I use it all day everyday and consider it one of the main reasons to use a Mac :man-shrugging:

text:
Regarding the bit about a hodgepodge of independent isolated apps, a lot of what divides apps and their data is just the way capitalism works being reflected in the platform. We have various roles, e.g., code editor, email client, and lots of different apps compete in that space against each other (Gmail, Superhuman, Outlook, <http://Mail.app|Mail.app>). Apps don't talk to each other, or use the same data structures, because they compete with each other. You can actually see theses walls breakdown when one company owns a bunch of apps, Microsoft Office, Adobe Creative Suite, and Affinity's apps all illustrate this with their interoperability features.

Any view of a more seamless/monolithic desktop that doesn't solve this problem will never work, because if apps can't compete with each other than the platform will stagnate.

Regarding the bit about a hodgepodge of independent isolated apps, a lot of what divides apps and their data is just the way capitalism works being reflected in the platform. We have various roles, e.g., code editor, email client, and lots of different apps compete in that space against each other (Gmail, Superhuman, Outlook, Mail.app). Apps don't talk to each other, or use the same data structures, because they compete with each other. You can actually see theses walls breakdown when one company owns a bunch of apps, Microsoft Office, Adobe Creative Suite, and Affinity's apps all illustrate this with their interoperability features.

Any view of a more seamless/monolithic desktop that doesn't solve this problem will never work, because if apps can't compete with each other than the platform will stagnate.

text: The browser companies never really got behind AppleScript. So once web apps became so dominant, AppleScript was no longer usable because you were missing a connection, and then the commercial apps like Zapier, etc., spring up to fill the void. Basically the industry dismantled the intra-program protocols as the web was ignoring them. The Web and the companies behind it like Google, Facebook, etc. is a very arrogant world that has the fundamental attitude that they are in control, and that other people better get out of the way.

text: Yeah, definitely that’s true that AppleScript never worked with the web, on the other hand the web has its own approach to interapp communication with APIs which seems to be working out alright? Google's stuff seems pretty scriptable, e.g., Google Sheets, Facebook not so much, but that’s definitely a business decision, not a technical one.

text: Interesting discussion! I agree with <@UEQ6M68H0> that external control of apps has disappeared mainly because today’s dominant players can afford not to offer it and have a commercial interest in creating silos and walled gardens. But we also lack the infrastructure, technical but most of all social and legal, for a market of small to mid-size players to develop interoperable components of non-trivial complexity. Software development is all bottom-up. You take existing stuff (dependencies) and build on them. There is no top-down design as it’s done for material goods, based on markets for standard components and subcontractors that produce to an agreed-on specification. Therefore complex software assemblies are fragile unless they come from the giants who control their full software stack.

text: I like to look at this from the bundling/unbundling strategy perspective as I think it’s a more complete view of what is happening. text: Another issue is that software developers obsess about code but don't think that much about data. In many situations we don't actually need to control an app from the outside, we merely want to access the data it handles. That goal would best be served by well designed data models and data formats, defined independently of libraries or applications.

Our key pillars are dependency injection and fakes. For example, printing to screen shouldn’t be written as: print(“hello”) It should be written: print(REAL_SCREEN, “hello”)

It should be both! Dependencies should be either injected like that or deduced from the environment itself - both is optimal.

So it should be (using names before blocks as “enviroment with this dependency runs the block”
Traces are a great idea for a ground-up feature of the stack and so are blocks for easier allocation, can’t wait to see the high level language you come up with for this.
